questions.question: Explain String immutability in Java. Why are Strings immutable and what are the advantages?
keyPoints: Strings are immutable - once created, content cannot change
Any modification creates a new String object
Advantages: thread safety, string pool optimization, security for class loading and network connections
Hash code caching for efficient use in HashMaps
Example demonstrating immutability with code

questions.question: Differentiate between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer with examples.
keyPoints: String: immutable, thread-safe, slower for modifications
StringBuilder: mutable, not thread-safe, faster for single-threaded use
StringBuffer: mutable, thread-safe (synchronized), slower than StringBuilder
Use String for constants, StringBuilder for single-thread manipulation, StringBuffer for multi-thread manipulation
Code examples showing each usage

questions.question: Explain the String pool concept in Java. How does it affect memory management?
keyPoints: String pool is a special memory area in the heap
String literals are stored in the pool and reused
new String() creates objects outside the pool on the heap
intern() method adds strings to the pool
Reduces memory usage by sharing string instances

questions.question: Write a Java program demonstrating various String methods: length(), charAt(), substring(), indexOf(), and toUpperCase().
keyPoints: length() returns character count
charAt(index) returns character at position
substring(begin, end) extracts portion of string
indexOf(str) returns position of first occurrence
toUpperCase() and toLowerCase() for case conversion
